name: Puntal de l'Aljub and Pico Bellota Loop
name_original: Parque Natural de la Serra d'Espadà — Trail run circular al Puntal de l'Aljub
description: This difficult 11.2-mile loop in Sierra Espadán offers challenging terrain, 3185 feet of elevation gain, and panoramic views.
region_intro: Jogging around Chóvar offers diverse terrain within the Sierra de Espadán Natural Park, characterized by its mountainous landscape and elevations between 400 and 800 meters. The region features abundant pine and centuries-old cork oak forests, providing scenic and shaded running routes. Distinctive red soils and natural springs, such as Fresca and Cerezo la Vaquera, contribute to the unique environment. The Ajuez Gorge and Reservoir also present varied paths for runners, from wider sections to steeper ascents. Best jogging routes around Chóvar The most popular jogging route is Puntal de l'Aljub and Pico Bellota Loop, an 11.2 miles (18.1 km) trail that takes 2 hours 55 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers significant elevation changes through forested areas. Another top favourite among local runners is Puntal de l'Aljub – View from Pico Peña Pastor loop from Eslida, a difficult 20.0 miles (32.3 km) path. This extensive route provides panoramic views over the mountainous landscape. Local runners also love the Coll Roig (Red Pass) – Puntal de l'Aljub loop from Eslida, an 11.6 miles (18.7 km) trail leading through cork oak forests and mountainous terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 15 minutes. Jogging around Chóvar is defined by mountainous terrain, ancient cork oak forests, and natural springs. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Chóvar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 16 reviews. More than 4000 runners have used komoot to explore Chóvar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Chóvar?
A: There are over 180 running routes around Chóvar, offering a wide range of options for all skill levels. You'll find everything from easy paths to challenging trails with significant elevation changes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging trails in Chóvar?
A: The trails in Chóvar are predominantly mountainous, with elevations ranging from 400 to 800 meters. You'll run through abundant pine and centuries-old cork oak forests, experience distinctive red soils, and encounter varied paths, including sections around the scenic Ajuez Gorge and Reservoir.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running routes in Chóvar?
A: While many routes in Chóvar are rated as difficult due to the mountainous terrain, there are a few easier options available. These typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, perfect for those new to trail running or looking for a more relaxed jog.

Q: Can I find circular jogging routes in the Chóvar area?
A: Yes, many of the running routes around Chóvar are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31783246" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Eslida – Matilde Spring loop from Eslida&lt;/a&gt; is a great option that takes you through varied landscapes.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while running in Chóvar?
A: Chóvar's routes offer a blend of natural beauty and historical interest. You can pass by the scenic Ajuez Gorge and Reservoir, enjoy views of ancient cork oak forests, and discover natural springs like Fresca and Cerezo la Vaquera. Historical landmarks include the ruins of a Muslim castle (&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2756864" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Castle&lt;/a&gt;) and the 17th-century Nevera de Castro snow well.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by historical sites or castles?
A: Absolutely. Many trails weave through areas rich in history. For instance, you might encounter the remains of ancient Muslim corrals or early 20th-century ovens from the old Cinabrio Mines. The ruins of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2756864" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Castle&lt;/a&gt;, perched on the highest part of the town, are also accessible from various routes, offering a glimpse into the region's past.

Q: Is Chóvar suitable for family-friendly jogging or running with children?
A: While many routes are challenging, some sections or shorter, flatter paths within the natural park can be suitable for families. Look for routes described as 'easy' or 'moderate' and consider the distance and elevation carefully to match your family's ability. The natural springs and picnic spots can also make for enjoyable stops.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Chóvar and the Sierra de Espadán Natural Park?
A: Generally, dogs are permitted on trails within the Sierra de Espadán Natural Park, but it's crucial to keep them on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other users. Always check specific park regulations or signage for any restricted areas, especially during nesting seasons or in sensitive ecological zones.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Chóvar?
A: The komoot community highly rates the running experience in Chóvar, with an average score of 4.3 stars. Runners often praise the diverse and challenging mountainous terrain, the beauty of the ancient cork oak and pine forests, and the refreshing natural springs. The well-maintained trails and panoramic views are also frequently highlighted.

Q: Is there public transport available to reach the running trails in Chóvar?
A: Public transport options directly to specific trailheads in Chóvar can be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules to Chóvar town itself. For greater flexibility in accessing various starting points for runs, driving and utilizing available parking is often the most convenient option.

Q: Where can I park my car when visiting the running routes in Chóvar?
A: Parking is generally available in and around the village of Chóvar. Many trailheads, especially those popular with hikers and runners, often have designated parking areas nearby. It's recommended to arrive early during peak times to secure a spot, particularly for routes that start directly from the village or well-known access points.

Q: What is the best time of year to go running in Chóvar?
A: The spring and autumn months are generally ideal for running in Chóvar. The temperatures are milder, and the natural landscape is vibrant. Summers can be hot, making early morning or late afternoon runs preferable, while winters are typically mild but can have cooler, wetter days, especially at higher elevations.
tips: From its 948 meters above sea level, the Puntal de l'Aljub offers very beautiful views of the Serra d'Espadà and the valleys of the La Plana region. The views may be somewhat disturbed by the antennas but you will surely find some good panoramic views!

faqs.question: Is the Puntal de l'Aljub and Pico Bellota Loop suitable for jogging?
faqs.answer: No, this loop is generally not recommended for jogging. The trail features very steep inclines, rocky paths, and sections that require careful footing and even hand assistance. It is best suited for experienced hikers seeking a challenging workout.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly rocky and steep, with continuous ascents and descents. You'll encounter a 'vertical path' in some sections, particularly on the ascent to Puntal de l'Aljub, where hands may be needed for assistance. Steady footing is essential throughout the route.

faqs.question: How difficult is this trail?
faqs.answer: This is a difficult trail, designed for experienced hikers. It involves significant elevation gain over a relatively short distance, with technical and strenuous sections. The continuous ups and downs between the two peaks add to its challenging nature.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: Despite its difficulty, the trail offers stunning panoramic views. From the peaks, you can see across the Sierra Espadán, with vistas extending to the sea in one direction and other mountains in the other. On clear days, you might even spot Valencia city and Peñagolosa in the distance from Pico Bellota.

faqs.question: Are there any specific landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route takes you to the summits of Puntal de l'Aljub and Pico Bellota (also known as Acorn Peak), both offering exceptional views. You'll also pass through beautiful pine and cork tree forests, see interesting rock formations, and catch glimpses of Eslida village and its castle ruins.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike the Puntal de l'Aljub and Pico Bellota Loop?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging nature of the trail, it's best to avoid the hottest summer months. Spring and autumn offer more moderate temperatures, making the strenuous ascents more manageable. Winter can also be suitable, but be prepared for potentially colder conditions at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: While specific parking isn't detailed, trailheads for this loop are typically accessed from nearby villages such as Eslida or Chóvar. Look for designated parking areas within these villages, which serve as common starting points for routes in the Sierra Espadán Natural Park.

faqs.question: Is the Puntal de l'Aljub and Pico Bellota Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The Sierra Espadán Natural Park generally allows dogs, but they must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Given the trail's rocky and steep terrain, ensure your dog is fit and accustomed to such conditions, and bring plenty of water for them.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Sierra Espadán Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Typically, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required for hiking in the Sierra Espadán Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and practice Leave No Trace principles to preserve the natural environment.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route overlaps with several other official trails. You'll find sections that are part of the Trans-Espadán, Eslida a Chóvar, and El Camí IT5TR8 El Camí del Sud: de Dénia a Castelló de la Plana, among others.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging hike?
faqs.answer: For this difficult loop, essential gear includes sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support, plenty of water (especially as there's no water available on the trail), snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), a map or GPS device, and appropriate layers of clothing for changing weather conditions. Trekking poles can also be very helpful on the steep sections.
